PREFACE

The information provided in these guidelines is to be read in conjunction with the Blackboard Courses MANAGEMENT OF CHILD SEX OFFENDERS 2019 and SEX OFFENDER MANAGEMENT 2019 and is an aide for Regional WA OIC’s to assist them in their role as case managers to effectively manage persons deemed reportable offenders as provided in the Community Protection (Offending Reporting) Act 2004 (CPOR Act).

The case manager role may include the registration, management and monitoring of all sexual offenders registered as reportable offenders (RO’s) and their compliance with their reporting obligations. The National Child Offender System (NCOS) is an Australia wide computer system utilised for the purpose of recording this highly confidential information.

The successful completion of both blackboard courses will result in an RMIS recognition; this accreditation is required prior to the Sex Offender Registry (Registry) facilitating your access to the NCOS formerly known as ANCOR.

To ensure you have a proficient understanding of the management of RO’s please review the SEX OFFENDER MANAGEMENT 2019 course on blackboard before enrolling in the MANAGEMENT OF CHILD SEX OFFENDERS course.

sections.t: What is the Sex Offender Management Squad (soms) and the Sex

sections.h:
All Australian police authorities register, manage and monitor convicted child sex offenders who are not in custody by utilising and sharing information via NCOS.

SOMS is responsible for the registration, ongoing management and monitoring of all reportable offenders within the metropolitan area. Regional WA OIC’S are delegated case managers and manage and maintain reportable offenders and dangerous sex offenders within regional districts of Western Australia.

SOMS supports the Department of Corrective Services in the management of Dangerous Sexual Offenders (DSOs) who breach their obligations pursuant to the Dangerous Sex Offenders Act 2006.

SOMS provide support to case managers and each regional district also has a Local District Manager (LDM) as a point of contact for advice and guidance regarding case management and NCOS.

All case managers are encouraged to pro-actively target high risk offenders and liaise with other agencies such as the Australian Federal Police (AFP) and the Department of Communities to assist in the effective case management of RO’s.

The Registry coordinates NCOS access, case file access and intrastate or interstate transfers. The Registry also facilitates the governance, audit and risk assessment of all case files. The Registry will provide administrative assistance and guidance when requested and also facilitates the Community Protection website for offenders who are published on the website.

This can be found under State Crime/Sex Crime Division/Sex Offender Registry webpage and includes the application form (refer page 45 of Appendices) to gain access to NCOS on the completion of the required blackboard training. Send your completed access form to the Registry email sex.offender.registry@police.wa.gov.au You can contact the Registry directly to obtain forms or advice on 1800 300 400. A copy of the most commonly used forms is attached to the back of this document.

Also located on the webpage is a Quick Link to the Community Protection (Offender Reporting) Act 2004 and Dangerous Sexual Offenders Act 2006.

sections.t: How to Access & Log Into Ncos
sections.h:
NCOS is a system owned and managed by Australian Criminal Intelligence Commission (ACIC). To navigate to the ACIC log on page for NCOS utilise the WA Police Intranet site. Select Internet Links/Useful Links Directory; choose CrimTrac Portal (authorised users only). From the resulting list displayed - National Systems - select NCOS – National Child Offender System (refer to page 43 of the Appendices for further guidance).

Your User ID will be the letter W followed by your PD number (e.g. W12345) then enter your password. Once successfully logged in, the system will take you to the homepage of NCOS.

After you have utilised the system, if you require a password reset, there is an option for a Self-service Password Reset. This allows you to reset you own password without the need to request one from your authorised contact. A valid email address is required and must be associated with your ACIC online account. A copy of this process is included in the Appendices on page 44 of this document.

Section 81 of the CPOR Act states the Register (NCOS) must only be accessed by a person who is authorised by the Commissioner of police. Authorised person means a police officer or a person who falls within a class as prescribed in the regulations.

Information release regarding the personal details of ROs held in the register are only to be disclosed by an authorised police officer of or above the rank of Inspector unless to a police officer or law enforcement agency.

Section 82 of the CPOR Act states a person must not directly, or indirectly, record, disclose or make use of any personal information in the Register except;

 In the course of an officers duty

 As required or authorised by or under the CPOR Act or another written law  For the purpose of proceedings relating to a protection order  With the written authority of the minister or the person to whom the information relates

 In other circumstances prescribed by Regulations

Section 82 MUST be read in conjunction with Section 81.

sections.t: Overview of the Management of Reportable
sections.h: OFFENDERS
sections.b: OFFENDERS

sections.h:
The CPOR Act was proclaimed on the 31 December 2004 and came into effect on the 1 February 2005 requiring certain persons convicted of child sex offences or other serious offences to register and report their personal details to keep police informed of their whereabouts for a defined period of time.

The CPOR Act was introduced to:-

 Reduce the likelihood that they will re-offend within the community  Assist in the investigation and prosecution of any future offences that they may commit

 To enable information to be published about certain offenders  To enable the courts to make orders prohibiting certain offenders from engaging in specified conduct and for related purposes
sections.b: The CPOR Act was proclaimed on the 31 December 2004 and came into effect on the 1 February 2005 requiring certain persons convicted of child sex offences or other serious offences to register and report their personal details to keep police informed of their whereabouts for a defined period of time. The CPOR Act was introduced to:-  Reduce the likelihood that they will re-offend within the community  Assist in the investigation and prosecution of any future offences that they may commit  To enable information to be published about certain offenders  To enable the courts to make orders prohibiting certain offenders from engaging in specified conduct and for related purposes

sections.h:
Reportable offences are divided into two classes as defined in section 10 and 11 of the CPOR Act and are detailed in Schedule 1 and 2 of this Act (refer pages 40-41 of this manual).

Class 1 offences include murder of a child, sexual penetration or any sexual offences against a child under the age of 16 years.

Class 2 offences are some non-contact sexual offences as well as indecent assaults, using electronic devices to record, communicate or procure a child and child exploitation material related offences.

The CPOR Act outlines the period of time a RO is required to comply with the reporting obligations. The reporting periods are 8 years for schedule 2 offences, 15 years for a schedule 1 offence or life if convicted of a second sexual offence against a child. The reporting period is half that period if the person was a child when they committed the offence.

Police manage reportable offenders for the following reasons:-  Ensure compliance with reporting obligations as outlined in the CPOR Act  Protect the community by preventing further offences

 Keep police informed of their whereabouts and other personal details  To enable court to make child protection orders (CPO)  To publish certain offenders on the Community Protection Website  Reduce the likelihood of reportable offenders re-offending Page | 10

 To facilitate the prosecution of any future offences

sections.h:
Section 6 of the CPOR Act states a reportable offender is a person whom a court, on or after a commencement date, is sentenced for a reportable offence.

Reportable offences are defined in Section 10 and 11, however, the Registry will generally make case managers aware when a person becomes a reportable offender within their district.

The Registry receives notification through the prosecution system advising that a person has been charged or convicted of a reportable offence – NCOS is updated to start the registration process.

Upon release from prison after serving the sentence or receiving a non-custodial sentence in court a person is served a P308 registration notice (refer Appendices page 50). P308’s are forwarded to the Registry to assist in the facilitation process on

NCOS.

The P308 advises the person they are a RO and outlines their legal obligations to contact police within 7 days as per Section 67 of the CPOR Act. The reverse side of the P308 has the legislative obligations imposed on the RO.

The Registry will create a case file on NCOS containing copies of the P308, IMS summary, criminal court outcomes, statement of material facts and other relevant information. The Registry will then transfer the case file to the relevant supervisor.

sections.h:
To start a new registration in NCOS select the new registration sub record tab across the top menu bar. Fill in the Local Reference number which is the SID number located in IMS. All fields that have a red astricts are mandatory details to be entered on NCOS. As each field is completed, simply select ‘save & next’ to proceed to the next field.

If the case manager receiving the initial registration does not have access to a computer during the interview a manual registration form may be completed to capture the reportable details. The manual registration form can be downloaded from the WA Police Intranet.

As the case manager ensure you are aware of the person’s criminal history, statement of material facts and any other relevant information or intelligence for the offence, in particular, any associations with children. Case managers are to check WA Police system holdings to ensure the RO has no outstanding warrants or matters which need further investigation. These checks are also expected to be completed prior to each and every NORO and form part of your pre-interview preparation.

Page | 11

When the RO has contacted police, the case manager must organise a suitable time for the RO to attend the nominated approved place to register under Section 34 of the CPOR Act.

The case manager registering the RO must take the following steps:- If a RO is a child or has a disability, they may nominate some other person to attend and make the report on the ROs behalf. This person may be a parent, guardian, carer of other independent person nominated by the RO as per Section 35.

Ensure all parts of the registration of the RO occur in a private office out of the hearing of the public. Under Section 36 the RO has the right to privacy during the registration process.

The authorised person receiving the report may arrange an interpreter to be present when a person is making a report; the interpreter has to sign an undertaking not to disclose information derived from the report as per Section 36.

The RO is entitled to be accompanied by a support person of his/her choosing.

Check 2 forms of identification provided by the RO as outlined in Section 38 and Regulation 15. This can be ID such as drivers’ licence, Medicare card and/or birth certificate. A passport must be included if the RO holds one. A photograph is to be taken of the ID and uploaded into NCOS as an attachment.

If the identification is insufficient or you still wish to verify the ROs identity, you may take the fingerprints as per Section 39. These fingerprints should be marked ‘NCOS Registration’ and sent to Forensic for processing.

Section 40 allows for a photograph to be taken of the RO – ideally this should be a passport style headshot. Photograph all tattoos, marks and scars – except any in the genital or buttocks area.

Under Section 26 – 31 of the CPOR Act a RO must report the following personal details to police upon registration:-

 Current or previous names

 Date of birth

 Passport details

 ** Residential address/locations or where they can be generally located where he or she resides for 7 days (whether consecutive or not) or more with a 12 month period.

 Contact telephone numbers

 *** Email addresses including communication or internet websites – including user names and passwords associated with those accounts  Internet service provider details

 The names and ages of children who generally reside in the same household or with whom they have regular unsupervised contact for at least 3 days, whether consecutive or not, in any 12 month period. Any addresses where the RO is present for 7 days or more within a 12 month period where any Page | 12

children reside for at least 3 days, whether consecutive or not, in any 12 month period

 Employment details if employed for 7 days or more within a 12 month period  Affiliation with clubs or organisations where children may be present  Vehicle details – make, model, colour and registration of any motor vehicle owned by or generally driven by him or her for 7 days or more within a 12 month period

 Tattoos, marks and scars including acquisition or removal  Intended or regular travel within WA, Australia or overseas  Any convictions for reportable offences in foreign jurisdictions  Any time spent in custody since being sentenced or released from custody in respect to reportable offences

** Address confirmation details will need to be completed in NCOS once the address has physically been checked and verified by the case manager preferably within 7 days of the address reported.

*** In relation to on-line accounts, the RO is offered a CONSENT TO ACCESS AND EXAMINE INTERNET ACCOUNTS form. Ensure the RO is fully aware it is completely voluntary whether or not they sign it and they can withdraw their consent at any time. Scan and attach the signed consent form as a FILE NOTE.

The final part of the registration process is the reporting details. This MUST be filled in correctly to ensure the correct reporting period is entered enabling NCOS to calculate the correct time the RO has to report according to the reportable offence as stated in part 3 divisions 5 of the CPOR Act. The notification date is the date the RO was served the P308 and the initial registration date is the date the offender completed the new registration with police.

As a matter of procedure provide a brief explanation to the RO of the pending reporting obligations upon them and the penalties that apply if they breach through non-compliance – this is to ensure the person understands what’s required of them as a RO. All of this information is on the back of the NORO and included in the YOUR REQUIREMENTS – REPORTABLE OFFENDER brochure which should also be served on the RO at registration.

Finally, all reportable offenders are issued a NORO at the completion of the registration advising next where to next report, including time, date and place and their reporting obligations in regards to personal details. After a new registration a RO will be required to report on a regular basis dependant on the risk level result.

It is advised to issue a NORO approximately 4 weeks after the initial registration. At the next NORO interview a risk assessment has been completed and reviewed by the Registry. To complete the NORO, fill in the ROs name and date of birth followed by the reporting period; this is the date the RO was released upon conviction from court or custody. Enter the case manager’s name and any additional case managers that the RO can report to. Using the drop down boxes, select the time, Page | 13

date and location (nearest police station if the RO resides or is located in regional

WA).

The RO and case manager are to sign the NORO, the original must be retained and uploaded in NCOS and a copy provided to the RO as per Section 67 of the CPOR Act.

Once the registration has been completed the new registration case file will be populated in NCOS creating two case files. The original one being created by the Registry upon conviction of the reportable offence. The Registry will merge both case files completing a quality assurance check to ensure information entered is acceptable resulting in one case file which will have the registration status of ‘Registered’.

sections.h:
OFFENDERS

The management of ROs is a complex task for WA Police, however, it is central to ensuring effective community protection.

The Registry along with SOMS have developed strategies to ensure that risks are identified and case management plans are developed, implemented and reviewed on a regular basis. The case management plan must include action to monitor the behaviour and attitudes of the RO and to intervene in their life where necessary in order to control and minimise the risk of serious harm.

The minimum reporting obligation is once per year in addition to providing details of any of the changes in their circumstances outlined in Division 2 of the CPOR Act.

However, a static risk assessment in conjunction with a dynamic risk assessment will assist case managers to determine how often Police should have contact with the

RO:

 VERY HIGH – ONCE A MONTH

 HIGH – EVERY 3 MONTHS

 MEDIUM – EVERY 6 MONTHS

 LOW – ONCE A YEAR

The risk assessments assist to evaluate the likelihood of the RO re-offending and is a key component in the on-going case management to assist in prioritising high risk ROs within the district. The risk assessment can aid in the decision making process surrounding the management of a RO. It can identify and monitor factors to reduce risks, ensure effective case management of limited resources and supports prioritisation.

sections.h:
To ensure effective case management it is important you understand your RO and to assess and obtain a profile. You can do this by asking questions or assessing factors relating to risk such as:-

Do they have repetitive behaviours for example do they use the similar methods for grooming different or multiple victims?

Do they have a certain target group of victim/s? Is their victim male/female? Do they prefer certain age groups?

Has the previous victim/s been interfamilial, friends or strangers?

Has violence been a part of the offence or have they become increasingly violent with each subsequent offence?

Were there any identifiable signs leading up to the offence? Such as unemployment, death in the family, relationship breakup or other situations which may reduce their self-esteem?

Was the offence related to alcohol or drug use?

As the RO who resides at the same address, who they regularly socialise with or what additional hobbies or interests they enjoy?

Regular asking or checking on dynamic factors about their lifestyle in interviews, home visits or general patrols may assist in identifying warnings signs – asking the RO questions such as:-

Do they reside near the victim(s)? Are the victims’ family likely to cause problems for the RO?

Do they reside near a school, playground or other area children are found – this is very relevant if they groom victims or they have this form of MO.

Do they have a good support network or do they have limited family or friends?

Did the complete any sex offender treatment program whilst in custody or do they intend to attend any self-help programs available to them within the community?

Do they have trouble forming long term intimate relationships with adults? Do they regularly utilise sex phone lines or prostitutes?

If they have had convictions for possession of child exploitation material do they have a computer at home? Can they access child exploitation material again?

If they had previous ‘cyber’ chat room contact with victims do they regularly access the internet/email? If so do they engage in chat rooms, dating sites, communication sites or attempt to engage potential victims online?

Gaining rapport with your RO will enable you to seek additional information as part of the risk assessment process.

Page | 15

As a case manager, if you identify the risk level of a RO that needs to be elevated or reduced, you must contact the Registry for approval prior to changing the risk status and the ongoing reporting regime. Once approval is granted, do not amend the level of risk in NCOS but add an alert in NCOS stating the following ‘Medium Risk of re- offending however currently managed as High Risk’.

All actions regarding the reporting regime or decisions around risk should be recorded in file notes in NCOS. The Case Management Guidelines are used as a guide to provide a structure to the dynamic environment that is managing reportable offenders.

The Registry are now utilising a new Dynamic Sexual Risk Assessment tool (SHARP

– refer Appendices page 80) that has been developed using years of statistical data

and experience from law enforcement agencies within Australia who deal with sex offenders. The SHARP has replaced Case Management Plans and is used for all VERY HIGH and HIGH ROs. The aim of SHARP is to prioritise ROs so case managers can informatively target those who pose the most risk to the safety of our community.

 SEXUAL DEVIANCE

 HISTORY OF SUPERVISION VIOLATION

 ANTI-SOCIALISM

 RISKY ENVIRONMENT

 PROTECTIVE & PROXIMAL MANAGEABILITY

The SHARP outlines the ROs criminal history and present and proximal risk factors.

These are used to help case managers identify manageability issues, areas where risk can be mitigated and reasons why a RO may be likely to reoffend. Further, it identifies protective factors which can be utilised in positive case management.

High risk factors pertaining to RO’s predominantly include:-  Sexual abuse on males

 Paedophilia (People at least 5 years older, who fantasise or are sexually aroused by prepubescent children under the age of 13 years old for more than 6 months). This is specifically of concern when the RO has committed sexual offences against more than 1 unrelated male victim.

 Proximal risk factors return (factors that attributed or were present when the RO initially offended).

 Sexual deviance (paraphilia, including bestiality and exhibitionism).

 Anti-social orientation and an apparent willingness to violate rules and norms, as well as take advantage of, and hurt others. (Can be seen by the RO committing other criminal acts, engaging in deceptive, irresponsible and impulsive behaviour).

SHARP is a dynamic/fluid tool that requires updating as pertinent information comes to light or any of the above high risk factors are newly or re-identified. The Registry are completing the assessments for all Regional WA ROs week by week as they are due to report for their NOROs. SHARP assessments will predominately be Page | 16

completed by the Registry, however, they require local case managers (ideally OICs) to complete the RISKY ENVIRONMENT & PROTECTIVE FACTORS sections on pages 4 & 5 of the document when you interview the RO.

All updates and information of interest are to be recorded either within the SHARP itself or on the running sheet at the bottom of the form. It is to be formally reviewed every 12 months. It will be saved on NCOS under the MANAGEMENT – RISK ASSESSMENT tabs, where it can be updated as necessary.

For more information the case management guidelines can provide:-  Definitions of various risk levels associated with ROs  Outlines the minimum standards for case management

 Recommends case management options that are relevant to the identifies risks

 Advice on maximising the effective allocation and use of resources related to case management of ROs

sections.h:
As per Section 67 of the CPOR Act a NORO states their reporting obligations and the time, date and place they are next required to report to police.

As stated on the NORO (refer Appendices page 57), in the event the persons listed are not available at the day/time of their arranged interview the RO must arrange an alternative interview time. Contact can be made by emailing the Registry or phoning the 1800 number; case managers will be emailed by the Registry and advised of any contact.

The NORO also states a report to a police station or to any other person not listed on the NORO does not constitute as compliance and is classed as a breach of reporting obligations.

The reverse side of the NORO states the reporting obligations and the time frames associated in making a report of changes to personal details. It also states the reporting obligations if the RO enters another state or territory as a corresponding offender.

INTERVIEWS – SECTION 37 RECEIPT OF INFORMATION

A Section 37 RECEIPT OF INFORMATION PROVIDED (refer Appendices page 59) must be used on all occasions upon contact with a RO. Interview notes must be taken to record any risk behaviours or additional information upon contact with the

RO.

A case manager must provide the RO with a copy of the Section 37 which records the change of details. The receipt must include the full name and date of birth of the Page | 17

person making the report; it must also include the name of the person receiving the information.

Fill in the required details regarding how the report is made by completing the:-  Date, time and location of the notification of change  Signature of the authorised person who received the report  The ROs signature to acknowledge the change was made

Section 37 of the CPOR Act states you must provide a unique reference number – this is provided in the same format as an incident report i.e. date/time/regimental number of the person receiving the report.

The original Section 37 and any notes must be retained and uploaded to NCOS. It is recommended to take additional interview notes relating to possible risk factors whilst conducting a NORO interview.

The case manager must ensure the change of details are reflected in NCOS within the ROs case file.

At the conclusion of the interview process, the following administrative steps are required on NCOS:-

First scan and save the NORO and Section 37 using the naming convections of SURNAME NORO DDMMYY and SURNAMES37/INTER NOTES DDMMYY.

In NCOS select the ‘Management’ tab then ‘Notifications’. Select ADD to upload the NORO. Select the BROWSE button to locate the NORO within the drive.

The NCOS alert ‘next reporting date not set’ will disappear, if this alert remains you may have entered in the date incorrectly.

sections.h:
NCOS

REPORTING CHANGES – SECTION 26(2) & 29 CPOR ACT

Section 26(2) of the CPOR Act states that a RO does not generally reside at any particular premises unless he/she resides at those premises for at least 7 days (whether consecutive or not) in any period of 12 months.

The same reporting obligations apply with all other personal details EXCEPT those where unsupervised contact or residing in the same household with a child occurs.

A RO does not have regular unsupervised contact with a child unless he/she has unsupervised contact with a child for a least 3 days (whether consecutive or not) in any period of 12 months.

These reporting obligations also apply if a RO changes their residential address or locality or if a child resides in the same household.

Page | 18

A RO MUST report any changes to his/her personal details within 24 hours if the change applies to unsupervised contact with a child or children for at least 3 days (whether consecutive or not) in any period of 12 months.

All other personal details MUST be changed within 7 days of that initial change occurring. For example, if a change occurs in employment for 7 days, then it must be reported within 7 days of the change, effectively making 14 days.

Failure to comply with these reporting obligations is a serious offence resulting in a breach of reporting obligations under section 63 of CPOR Act.

REPORTING CHANGES IN PERSON – SECTION 35 CPOR ACT

Reporting the following changes of personal details MUST be done in person:-  Change of address or general locality

 Acquisition or removal of tattoos, marks and scars

 And any other details such as return to Western Australia from Interstate or overseas along with relevant documentation to confirm travel/accommodation It is advised as procedure to take any reports of contact with children in person.

The report can be made at any police station in the locality of where the RO currently resides or at another place approved – this report must be received by the case manager, authorised person or police officer.

Section 37 of the CPOR Act states as soon as practicable after receiving a report, the authorised person must acknowledge the making of the report, the acknowledgement must be in writing and given to the person making the report.

This is completed by providing the RO with a Section 37 which records the change of details.

Failure to comply with these reporting obligations is a serious offence resulting in a breach of reporting obligations under section 63 of CPOR Act. REPORTING CHANGES IN PERSON – SECTION 35 CPOR ACT Reporting the following changes of personal details MUST be done in person:-  Change of address or general locality  Acquisition or removal of tattoos, marks and scars  And any other details such as return to Western Australia from Interstate or overseas along with relevant documentation to confirm travel/accommodation It is advised as procedure to take any reports of contact with children in person. The report can be made at any police station in the locality of where the RO currently resides or at another place approved – this report must be received by the case manager, authorised person or police officer. Section 37 of the CPOR Act states as soon as practicable after receiving a report, the authorised person must acknowledge the making of the report, the acknowledgement must be in writing and given to the person making the report. This is completed by providing the RO with a Section 37 which records the change of details.

sections.h:
All other personal details can be reported electronically via email to SOMS station mail or in the case of Regional WA, email the Registry. Do not receive a report if the RO has emailed the case manager directly.

If changes are reported electronically the authorised person must respond confirming the change has been accepted and quote a unique reference number as a legislative obligation under Section 37.

Submit a running sheet entry of the email contact with the RO regarding the changes of their personal details into file notes in NCOS. A copy of the sent email is to be saved and attached as a file note with the heading of the file note ‘Email change of details’ stating the change subject for example address or vehicle.

sections.h:
Within Western Australia

Sections 29(A) – 32 of the CPOR Act state the reporting obligations imposed for a RO in regards to intended travel arrangements.

In accordance with section 29A an RO must report any intention to leave the place they generally reside for 7 or more consecutive days.

 At least 7 days before leaving their place of residence the RO must provide:-  Approximate dates which he/she intends to be absent

 Each address of location at which he/she intends to reside and the dates they intend to reside at those addresses/locations; and

 A RO travelling within WA is not required to report their return to the address they generally reside

If circumstances arise making it impractical for a RO to make the report at least 7 days before, it is sufficient compliance if the RO reports the required information no later than 24 hours after leaving.

Outside Western Australia

In accordance with Section 30 CPOR Act a RO must report any intention to travel outside of WA whether to travel elsewhere in Australia or to travel out of Australia.

At least 7 days before leaving their place of residence the RO must provide:-  Each state or territory he/she intends to visit

 Dates or approximate dates which h/she intends to stay in each state or territory

 Each address of location within each state or territory and the dates he/she intends to reside at those addresses/locations

 Date or approximate date he/she intends to return to WA  If he/she does not intend to return to WA

If circumstances arise making it impractical for a RO to make the report at least 7 days before, it is sufficient compliance if the RO reports the required information no later than 24 hours after leaving.

sections.h:
Purpose

On 13 December 2017, three new measures came into effect at the Commonwealth level to prevent registered child sex offenders with reporting obligations from travelling overseas without permission from a relevant authority:

 A new Commonwealth offence for registered offenders who travel, or attempt to travel, overseas without permission (maximum penalty of five years imprisonment)

 Implementation of a border alert by the Department of Immigration and Border Protection to stop registered offenders at the border, and  A scheme for the cancellation of passports held by registered offenders by the Minister for Foreign Affairs

The Passports Legislation Amendment (Overseas Travel by Child Sex Offenders) Act (Cth) 2017 (Passports Act) requires the Minister for Foreign Affairs to cancel or refuse to issue a passport to an Australian citizen who is a registered offender (with reporting obligations) upon request from a competent authority. A competent authority means a person with powers, functions or duties in relation to a child sex offender register. The relevant competent authority will be different in each jurisdiction but could include Police Commissioners and their delegates.

The Passports Act permits a RO to travel overseas if a competent authority has given permission for the person to leave Australia.

These Guidelines provide a suggested approach for competent authorities to consider requests for travel from registered offenders. The Guidelines have been developed by the Commonwealth Attorney-General’s Department to assist states and territories in considering travel exemptions under the passports measure.

Overseas travel principles

 The passports measure is designed to prevent registered child sex offenders from travelling overseas to sexually exploit or sexually abuse vulnerable children in overseas countries where the law enforcement framework is weaker and their activities are not monitored: Indonesia, Thailand, Philippines, Cambodia, Vietnam, Malaysia, Fiji and Papua New Guinea.

ROs subject to reporting obligations must obtain written permission from a competent authority before they may lawfully travel overseas. It is a Commonwealth offence for a registered offender to leave, or attempt to leave, Australia without such permission.

Requests for overseas travel by a RO should only be approved in justifiable circumstances and travel approval should typically be granted a limited period of time. Overseas travel for the purpose of a holiday should not generally be approved.

Applications should be considered on their individual merits, taking into account the purpose of child sex offence registers and related reporting obligations, in particular, ensuring the safety and protection of children by reducing the likelihood that a RO will reoffend. Consideration of applications should also take into account the reasons for the request and whether the proposed travel may be delayed until the conclusion of the RO’s reporting period.

Page | 21

Applications for permission to travel

A request for overseas travel must be made in writing (Overseas Travel Application Form) by the RO to the relevant competent authority in their jurisdiction, including detailed information about the grounds on which the RO is seeking to travel overseas and any documentation required to support the application. It is the responsibility of the RO seeking travel permission to provide all relevant material to assist the competent authority with making a determination.

The RO should also provide information on the management of any risk to children during their trip (for example, if during the travel they visit or stay with family members where children also reside).

In the normal course of events, applications should be made at least two months before the intended travel period unless the particular circumstances of the proposed travel do not allow such time. In urgent cases, the RO should indicate clearly in the application documentation the timeframe by which a decision is sought.

In considering an application, the competent authority may seek further information from the applicant or seek comments on the claims made in the application from relevant agencies (for example, police or prosecution authorities). The application and any supporting material provided may be supplied to the relevant agencies as part of this process.

The relevant competent authority should provide written confirmation to the RO of the outcome of their request for overseas travel. ROs should be advised not to apply for a passport or make any travel arrangements before written permission to travel is granted by the competent authority. A passport cannot be issued by the Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade unless the competent authority has provided approval for the travel.

Decision making criteria

In determining an application for overseas travel, the competent authority may take into account:

 the seriousness of the RO’s reportable offences

 the period of time since the offences were committed

 the age of the RO and the age of the victims at the time the offences were committed

 the RO’s current age

 the nature and seriousness of the RO’s full criminal record  the extent to which the RO has complied with reporting obligations  any intelligence information or assessment of the level of risk to children posed by the RO

 whether the proposed travel will involve any contact (planned or anticipated) with children and, if so, how any risk to children could be mitigated Page | 22

 to the extent that it relates to the requested travel—the circumstances of the RO, including their accommodation, employment needs and proposed travel companions

 the country or countries to which the RO seeks to travel, and  any other matter the competent authority considers relevant In addition, the competent authority may take into account the reasons and duration of the proposed travel. Circumstances that may provide a basis for a travel application could include (but are not limited to) the following grounds:

 Health care

- To receive a health treatment which is only available overseas

- To facilitate an overseas health treatment for a dependant

 Family reasons

- To visit a sick or dying relative who resides overseas

- To attend a funeral/wedding

 Employment/business purposes

- Current employment requires travel

- RO would be at a financial or professional loss if travel is not allowed

 Education

- To complete a mandatory requirement of an Australian course or

qualification

- To obtain a qualification that is not available in Australia

 Religious reasons

- Pilgrimage

If a RO applies for a passport or a passport renewal the application automatically gets placed on hold. DFAT then enquire with SOR as to whether or not an International Travel Application has been submitted by the RO (through SOMS or case managers at their local station) and has been approved by the Commissioner.

If an application has not been submitted and approved SOR Review Board will not support the RO holding a current Australian passport. DFAT are advised and the Immigration Minister will approve the decision to decline the passport application.

SOR then advise the RO by via their declared email address according to NCOS. If the RO still holds a current passport they are advised by letter (to their NCOS address) it is cancelled and to be surrendered.

The reason that passports are being cancelled and renewal applications refused is that there is no current requirement for a RO to hold a passport for identification purposes. Further, if no International Travel Application has been submitted and approved it is against the law for a RO to leave Australia.

Page | 23

Return Travel

Section 31 states a RO who has travelled outside of WA must as soon as practicable report any changes to travel details previously provided. Section 32 applies to a RO who was required to report under Section 30 that he/she intended to leave WA.

He/she MUST report their return to WA within 7 days of entering WA.

If the travel was interstate or overseas a RO is required to provide their passport and any other documentation indicating where they have travelled. In addition to this section, if a RO decides not to leave, the RO must report their decision not to leave within 7 days after deciding not to leave.

If intentions are to reside in another Australian state or territory other than WA for 7 days or more, the RO must report their presence to that state or territory Registrar.

Failure to report is a breach of reporting obligations under section 63 CPOR Act.

Travel Alert

To view a travel ALERT in NCOS select the TRAVEL tab. Here you will see the travel alert. First is the Travel Alert ID and Pace Reference No. Next is the status of the Alert and below is the period the alert is valid for. On the bottom part of the alert is the Overseas Advise/offence description and relevant contact details for SOMS.

sections.h:
Address confirmations should be completed as soon as the new address has been reported by conducting an unannounced home visit (UHV). Once the address has been verified tick the relevant boxes in the confirmation date and case manager’s details.

The address must be physically checked and attended by the case manager.

Sighting a piece of mail is not acceptable, the case manager should confirm the RO actually resides at the reported address.

Once the address has been verified tick the relevant boxes in the confirmation date and case manager’s details. The address must be physically checked and attended by the case manager. Sighting a piece of mail is not acceptable, the case manager should confirm the RO actually resides at the reported address.

sections.h:
An unannounced home visit (UHV) provides the case manager with an opportunity to check other personal information, yield information about anybody else residing at the address and provides the case manager with an opportunity to assess how or where the RO is living.

It is believed to be a strong deterrent for the RO which reinforces direct contact with police.

Page | 24

Case managers do not have power of entry to a premises without warrant. However, police should still undertake UHV as required to observe proximity to schools, children etc. The case manager can verify the address and any vehicles located at the address.

The UHV should be recorded in the ‘Management’ tab in ‘File Notes’. Select ‘Add’ and enter the date and case manager’s details with the naming convention UHV. In the comments box enter a brief description for example RO was at home, who was there, state of the house, what was discussed etc.

sections.h:
File notes should be utilised as a running sheet to record contact with the RO on every occasion; e-mails, telephone calls, UHV or management notes.

CHILD SAFETY – POSSIBLE CHILD AT RISK

WA Police have statutory obligations to disclose information to the Department for Child Protection and Family Support under the Children and Community Services Act 2004. This Act operates on the principle that the safety and wellbeing of a child or young person is the primary consideration.

Under the CPOR Act a RO is required to report unsupervised contact with a child over 3 days consecutive or not over a 12 month period. If a case manager identifies a possible child at risk, under Section 28 of the Children and Community Services Act 2004 a referral is to be released to CPFS.

The referral should be completed on an ‘Information Release – Possible Child at Risk’ referral which can be downloaded from the Police Intranet – Sex Offender Registry.

Save the form so you can edit the details. Enter all the required information regarding the RO, the details of the child and provide a brief description to justify the reasons as to why the case manager has identified a possible child at risk and the release of information.

The referral form is an information release which MUST be signed off by a police officer of or above the rank of Inspector who is authorised according to Section 81 and 82 of the CPOR Act.

You can NOT disclose any information regarding the risk assessment of a RO or any information which has been received from a third party. If required it is optional to release criminal court outcomes or the statement of material facts surrounding the reportable offence. In accordance with their own police and procedure CPFS would Page | 25

then either consider or undertake an assessment to determine any necessary course of action.

A six week bring up date should be entered in NCOS to alert the case manager to contact CPFS to find out the result of the assessment undertaken by CPFS. In NCOS in the ROs case file, under the ‘Management’ tab, to ‘Next Reporting Date Details’ and select the ‘Add’ button. Enter the case manager’s details, select the action type ‘Warning’ and enter a date 6 weeks after the referral has been sent to

CPFS.

Once six weeks expires, this allows for a system generated alert to remind the case manager to contact CPFS to assess if there have been any actions as a result of the referral. Safety plans put in place by CPFS can affect the RO case management and reporting obligations so it’s important to record any actions in file notes.

A copy of the referral sent to CPFS should be uploaded into ‘File Notes’ for future reference using the naming convention SURNAME CPFS REFERRAL DDMMYY.

If a case manager is unsure about whether a particular child/children may be at risk of harm they should discuss the matter further with their supervisor, the CPFS helpline or contact the Registry. Further, police are to comply with mandatory reporting obligations should a sexual incident involving a child be suspected.

Submission of a detailed IMS IR is required. Any immediate risks or concerns to children MUST be reported via the mandatory protocols currently in place.

sections.h:
FAILURE TO COMPLY – SECTION 63 CPOR ACT

A RO who, without reasonable excuse, fails to comply with any of his/her reporting obligations commits a crime. A breach of reporting obligations is a serious offence and can be as a result of failing to attend a scheduled NORO or failure to advise change of personal details.

In determining whether a person had a reasonable excuse, a court must have regard to:-

 The person’s age

 Whether that person has a disability that affects the person’s ability to understand or to comply with those obligations

 Whether the form of notification give to the RO was adequate to inform him/her having regard to the offender’s circumstances

 Any matter specified by the regulations for the purposes of this section; and Page | 26

 Any other matter the court considers appropriate

It is a defence to proceedings for an offence of failing to comply with a reporting obligation if it is established that, at the time of the offence is alleged to have occurred, the person had not received notice and was otherwise unaware of the obligation. A defence of this kind can occur when a RO has not been served a P308 upon release from custody or court upon sentence of a reportable offence.

sections.h:
*** INSPECTOR APPROVAL REQUIRED TO COMMENCE A PROSECTION ***

POWER TO DETAIN – SECTION 72 CPOR ACT

Section 72 enables the power to detain a RO if there are reasonable grounds to suspect that a person has not been given notice or is otherwise unaware of his/her reporting obligations. In this circumstance an alert on IMS must be placed stating Section 72 to enable an officer to detain for the purposes of serving a NORO.

For a person yet to be registered, officers are to detain the person to make arrangements for the registration of the person. An after-hours registration can be performed remotely by an authorised person. If an authorised person is unavailable to perform the registration, the initial details for the person are to be obtained and a NORO must be served. All police officers can serve a NORO if the RO is subject of a Section 72 alert.

In this circumstance an alert on IMS must be placed stating Section 72 to enable an officer to detain for the purposes of serving a NORO. An after-hours registration can be performed remotely by an authorised person. If an authorised person is unavailable to perform the registration, the initial details for the person are to be obtained and a NORO must be served. All police officers can serve a NORO if the RO is subject of a Section 72 alert.

sections.h:
If immediate inquiries (within 48 hours) do not result in contact with the RO, initiate an IMS incident report for Breach of Reporting Obligations. On any occasion that initiates an investigation which result in RO ‘personal details’ being placed on WA Police systems, approval is required from an authorised officer of or above the rank of Inspector. The approval is to be recorded on the IMS IR running sheet.

Upon creation of an IMS IR the case manager must:-

 Attach the relevant breached NORO as an exhibit of evidence  Attach a statement regarding the confirmation of the breach as a linked attachment

 Attach a blank NORO for service; and

 Attach an ‘Instruction for Service of NORO’ form with the step by step guide to serving a NORO (form located on the Police Intranet Site).

If a RO has failed to attend a NORO a wanted for questioning alert should be placed on IMS advising an officer that comes into contact with the RO what actions are required. The alert must contain the case managers or an afterhours contact.

Under ‘Actions’ the alert must:-

Page | 27

 Explain the purpose of the alert, such as ‘detain and interview for breach of reporting obligations under section 63 of the CPOR Act’  Reflect the statement of material facts in the IR narrative  Direct the officer to the narrative for the events surrounding the alert  Explain where to locate the NORO and the instructions to serve a NORO; and  If required, advise the officer they can detain the RO under Section 72 for the purpose to serve a notice or NORO

Once a RO has been located and charged accordingly, the IMS alert must be removed and an email sent to the Registry to ensure the RO is no longer published on the Community Protection Website.

Ensure all inquiries and results are updated on both NCOS and IMS.

Section 64 CPOR Act provides a person who, in purported compliance provides information that the person knows to be false or misleading in a material particular commits a crime. A Section 64 breach is to be dealt with in the same manner as a Section 63 breach (refer above).

PUBLICATION ELIGIBILITY – SECTION 63 CPOR ACT

It is important to identify a RO who has failed to comply as they can be eligible for publication as a missing offender on the Community Protection Website managed by the Registry. A Tier 1 checklist initiates the publication of the missing offender once he/she is deemed whereabouts unknown for failing to attend a NORO.

Case managers must ensure the RO cannot be found at their known locations prior to submitting a Tier 1 checklist. The checklist can be located on the Intranet under the Registry and is completed by the case manager and emailed to the Registry station mail preferably within 7 days of the breach of reporting.

sections.t: s.557K CC — Child Sex Offender, Offences by
sections.h:
Section 557K of the Criminal Code (refer Appendices page 92) has provisions for police to prosecute certain activities of convicted child sex offenders. Subsection 4 allows police to prosecute convicted child sex offender who, after having formally been warned by police, continue to consort habitually and regularly with another convicted child sex offender/s. Consort includes to communicate in any manner.

If you identify a RO who requires to be warned by police, contact the Registry to supply you with a consorting notice. A consorting notice is served by police on both ROs; once both have signed the notice it is retained and uploaded onto NCOS. If Page | 28

the ROs continue to consort, the charge of consorting can be preferred and is a Schedule 2 offence according to the CPOR Act.

557K subsection 5 allows the defence of persons consorting, if they are a spouse or a de facto partner or a de facto child or a lineal relative.

557K subsection 6 allows prosecution of convicted child sex offenders who, without a reasonable excuse, are in or near a public place where children are present.

THE COMMUNITY PROTECTION WEBSITE – PERSONS

sections.h:
The Community Protection Website is a limited online public register that allows for the publication of certain offenders under Section 85 CPOR Act.

The Commissioner of Police can release and publish information to the community concerning certain categories or ROs, dangerous sex offenders (DSOs) and other persons considered to be a risk to the lives or sexual safety of other persons. A missing offender allows the publication of personal details and the last known location of the ROs whose whereabouts are deemed unknown.

A local search allows a member of the public to view the dangerous and high risk offenders within their locality. It will publish a photograph of the offender but no other personal details are released.

The disclosure scheme enables a parent or guardian to apply to be informed if a person specified in the application is a RO who has unsupervised access to children.

This is not applicable if this person is a child.

sections.h:
Under Section 85F CPOR Act missing offenders can be published.

In most circumstances missing offenders are published for one or more of the following:-

 Failing to comply with any of his/her reporting obligations (e.g. failure to attend

NORO)

 Providing false or misleading information to police regarding reporting obligations

 Whereabouts are deemed unknown to police

The steps required to publish have already been covered in the Breach Process above.

Page | 29

Under Section 85I for Missing Offenders, prior to publication, the Commissioner must have consideration of the following:-

 Investigations or operations the RO is subject to

 The ROs compliance with current reporting obligations  Any orders under a written law the person is subject to  Identifying the victim their school and/or any other effect on the victim  Will it increase the risk of the RO offending?

 Will it identify a RO and prejudice a fair trial? and  Assessment of benefits to the community in publishing Apprehension of a missing RO means immediate response to un-publish the RO; the case manager must ensure the Registry is notified and NCOS and IMS updated.

sections.h:
The Registry maintains the local search facility once an offender becomes eligible for publication.

By generating a local search, the public can view the photograph and general locality of offenders who are high risk ROs, dangerous sex offenders and those who pose a risk to the Community within a locality.

The applicant completes the local search application, this verifies the applicant’s details within IMS. A link containing the requested details is sent to the applicants email address, this link is active for 24 hours.

Disclosure of information and confidentiality warnings continuously advise the applicant that severe penalties apply should an applicant release information to another source identifying the offender. The photograph will have the NCOS ID number of the person and the name of the applicant across the photo.

Legislative requirements under Section 85G subsection (6) require that prior to publication of high risk sex offenders, the following considerations:-  Is the offender a RO or DSO?

 That the offender is not a child

 The offender does not have a suppression order that prevents publication of details

 The offender has been given written notice of the proposal and reasons for disclosure; and

 At least 21 days has passed to allow a submission objection of the publication as per Section 85G subsection (3) of the CPOR Act.

If no considerations are addressed the offender will remain published on the website until their reporting obligations end.

Page | 30

sections.h:
The community protection disclosure scheme is available to any parent or guardian of a child/children. Under Section 85J the applicant can apply to the Commissioner to be informed whether or not a person, other than a juvenile, who has regular unsupervised contact with their child, is a RO.

However, under Section 85J subsections (3) to (5) prior to disclosure, the Commissioner must be satisfied by any evidence in support of the application, that the specified person has regular unsupervised contact with the applicant’s child for at least 3 days, whether consecutive or not, in any 12 month period.

The applicant will be required to provide their personal details, the details of the child/children and sufficient detail to identify the person of interest. If satisfied the person in question has regular unsupervised contact with a child, the Commissioner may inform the parent or guardian whether or not the specified person is a RO.

The applications are processed by the Registry who will inform the applicant.

Sections 85L and M are the sections provided to prevent persons conduct intended to incite or cause animosity or harassment to an identified offender. A member of the public cannot display or distribute any information identifying a person published on the Community Protection Website.

sections.h:
The Dangerous Sexual Offenders Act 2006 (DSO Act) was proclaimed on 13 May 2006 with the purpose to provide for detention in custody or the supervision of persons of a particular class to ensure adequate protection for the community.

The DSO Act also provides for the continuing control, care or treatment of persons of a particular class. This particular class of offender is referred to in Section 8 of the DSO Act, stating the DPP may file with the Supreme Court an application for orders in relation to a person or offender who:-

 Is under a custodial sentence for a serious sexual offence; or  Has been under a custodial sentence for a serious sexual offence and who, at all times since being discharged from the sentence, has been under a custodial sentence for another offence or offences.

Section 7 provides the power for the court to appoint the detention, continued detention or strict supervision in the community of certain persons deemed to be of a serious danger to the community.

It is the role of the DPP to make a DSO application. However, police do provide information to the DPP to assist in making the DSO order application. The court has to be satisfied that there is an unacceptable risk that if the person were not subject to a continuing detention order or a supervision order, the person would commit a Page | 31

serious sexual offence and to a high degree of probability that the person is a ‘serious danger to the community’ as defined in Section 7 of the DSO Act.

sections.h:
The court may order that a DSO is detained, remains in custody or order that a DSO be subject to specific supervision conditions such as a supervision order whilst in the community.

Supervision orders are under the provision of Section 17 and are managed by the Department of Justice. Supervision orders contain restrictive conditions on the offender such as attendance to regular psychiatric treatments, curfew checks, drug and alcohol testing and other restrictions based on their offending behaviours.

Continuing detention orders are reviewed by the court every 2 years; the court may rescind the detention order, if it is satisfied the community will be adequately protected.

All DSOs are fitted with an electric monitoring device for the purpose of enabling the person’s location to be monitored in conjunction with having exclusion zones as per their individual supervision order conditions.

WA Police and the Department of Justice work together to monitor and respond to breaches of the tracking devises and are monitoring 24/7. The level of response varies for WA Police where there is significant risk, for example the DSO cuts off their electronic monitoring device or has entered an exclusion zone.

It is the role of police to process any breaches identified through management by DCS or the DSO team located at SOMS.

MANAGING DSOs WITHIN YOUR COMMUNITY

DSOs that are also deemed ROs under the CPOR Act will be registered as per legislative obligations and standard operating procedures.

The DSOs that are not ROs are still required to report to a case manager as per the conditions of the supervision order and supply personal details as required.

 Once released into the community, a DSO is monitored by DoJ with the assistance of police supporting the management of DSOs by:-  Formulating case management plans and extraction plans for very high risk offenders

 Regular reporting to police and unannounced home visits – these may include random breath tests and curfew checks

 Proactively targeting DSOs when their risk factors increase; and  Assisting DoJ in the investigation of breaches or supervision orders under Section 40A of the DSO Act

Page | 32

If you are a case manager located in regional WA you will be notified prior to the release of a DSO within your community to what is expected of you. DSOs residing in regional WA will be managed by DoJ but are supported by a regional case manager. Further inquiries relating to the management of DSOs within your district should be forwarded to the Registry.

sections.t: Overview of Breaches of Dso Act 2006 (section 4OA & 21)
sections.h:
Breaches can be detected by DoJ but are enforced by police – although breaches are a simple offence if there is an evaluation of risk or pattern of behaviour then the DSO will be placed back into custody while the courts provide an outcome and amend the order.

Section 21 allows a member of the police or community corrections who reasonably suspects that a person who is subject to a supervision order is likely to contravene, is contravening, or has contravened a condition order, apply to a magistrate for the issue of a summons or warrant.

Pursuant to Section 23(b), if a Supreme Court is satisfied that there is an unacceptable risk that the DSO who is subject to the supervision order, would commit a serious offence, make a continuing detention order.

Section 40A states a person subject to a supervision order who, without reasonable excuse, contravenes a requirement of the order, commits an offence. This Section provides the power of arrest upon suspicion on reasonable grounds that a person has committed an offence such as contravening the supervision order.

This Section provides the power of arrest upon suspicion on reasonable grounds that a person has committed an offence such as contravening the supervision order.

sections.h:
INFORMATION

OVERVIEW OF CHILD PROTECTION ORDERS – CPO

Section 87 of the CPOR Act states the Commissioner of Police may apply to a court for a child protection order prohibiting a RO from engaging in specified conduct requiring compliance of an order.

Section 90 states the court may make the CPO only if the court is satisfied that the person is a RO and that the person poses a risk to the lives or sexual safety of one or more children and that making the order will reduce that risk.

A CPO may only be applied for to prevent a RO from specific conduct. A CPO may, in addition to or instead of prohibiting conduct, impose requirements on a RO as appropriate to reduce risk.

Page | 33

Section 94(a) may require a RO to comply with the orders of the Commissioner as to undergo assessment by a medical practitioner, a psychiatrist, a psychologist or social worker for appropriate treatment. A CPO can be applied for at any time whilst the RO is registered, even whilst in custody.

An affidavit of the specified conditions of the CPO are to be produced by the case manager which are then heard in a relevant court for a civil proceeding with the support of the State Solicitors Office.

As a case manager if you have identified a RO that you may think is a risk and could be eligible for a CPO, contact the Registry who will be able to assist in the application process.

Section 110A of the CPOR Act enables the Commissioner by notice in writing, to direct any public authority to provide on or before the day specified any information held by the public authority to assist with management of a RO or the determination of a CPO.

An application of this kind may be requested for the imposition of a particular order as outlined in Section 110a (1). In this circumstance a Section 110A is utilised to obtain information for the purposes of an order.

PAST OFFENDER REPORTING ORDER – PORO

The Commissioner may apply to a court for a Past Offender Reporting Order

(PORO) under Section 14, to enable a person deemed to be a RO for the purpose of

complying with the reporting obligations of the CPOR Act.

Person to whom an order can apply:-

 A person has been sentenced before the commencement day of an offence, and

 The person is not and has not been a RO in respect of that offence  That there currently exists a potential risk if this person is not subject to supervision as a RO

As a case manager, if you have identified a RO that you may think is high risk and would be eligible for a PORO, contact the Registry who will be able to assist in the application process.

A written submission collating the evidence and intelligence, an overview of the offence and the risk associated to support the PORO application must be written preferably by the case manager. This report will be assessed and considered by the Registry.

Page | 34

OFFENDER REPORT ORDER – SECTION 13 ORDERS

Section 13 states if a court finds a person guilty of an offence that is not a Class 1 or Class 2 offence; and the offence would not result in the person becoming a RO that court may order the offender to comply with reporting obligations under CPOR Act.

An Affidavit outlining the application is to be produced by the case manager which is heard in the relevant court. The court may make the order at the time the person is sentenced for the offence; within 6 months, after the person is sentenced or within 6 months after the person ceases to be in government custody in respect of the offence.

Please contact the Registry if you identify a person that may be eligible for a Section 13 order.

sections.h:
Section 80C of the CPOR Act states a RO must not apply to WA of the interstate Register to change their name without prior written approval from the Commissioner.

Failure to apply for change of name through the correct process is classed as a breach of reporting obligations under Section 80C of the CPOR Act.

PACE ALERTS – GREEN NOTICES & OTHER TRAVEL RELATED MATTERS

A Green Notice is issued to provide warnings and criminal intelligence about persons who have committed criminal offences and are likely to repeat these crimes in other countries.

It is an effective way to share key police intelligence on a global scale and to prevent offenders from crossing borders. The Green Notice acts as a warning, however, they do not indicate a person is wanted or the country on whose behalf the Green Notice is published.

A Green Notice is issued by INTERPOL upon request when:-  Person considered a threat to public safety

 Likely to commit a criminal offence

 Child sex tourism, Drug Trafficking, Fraudsters, Organised Crime members In Australia most notices are issued for child sex tourism.

As a case manager, you may identify persons that your suspect has suspicious travel arrangements, if so, contact the Registry who will submit a Green Notice application to INTERPOL for assessment. Upon approval of the Green Notice submission, the report will be returned to the Registry with a reference number. The Green Notice reference number will be added to the ROs case file in NCOS.

Page | 35

Upon approval of the Green Notice submission, the report will be returned to the Registry with a reference number. The Green Notice reference number will be added to the ROs case file in NCOS. sections.t: Requests for Information on the Register
sections.h:
For the purposes of Section 81 of the Act, the register is accessible by authorised persons only. Any information received from an external agency or person should be treated as sensitive material and protected accordingly.

If a police officer wishes to obtain information from NCOS, they must submit a request to the Registry. Information supplied for the purpose of NCOS must not be placed or updated in any police systems. For example, if a RO advises a change of address this address must not be changed within their IMS record.

If a case manager is disclosing information on a General Information Release, Inspectors approval is required. It there is an urgent need to advise the requesting agency and a police officer of the rank of Inspector or above is unavailable to sign the form, approval can be provided by email or telephone. In both circumstances a record of the approval and a formal signature is to be obtained in a timely manner.

The form must be saved, scanned and uploaded in NCOS in the ROs case file within file notes using the correct naming convention. The original form must be retained and placed in the ROs hard copy case file contained at the Registry.

To initiate a release of information, download the ‘General Information Release’ form from the registry intranet site and follow the prompts on the form.

sections.t: Suspension of Case Files in Ncos
sections.h:
ROs case files can be suspended within NCOS to reflect a suspension in reporting obligations.

In accordance with Section 44 of the CPOR Act a ROs reporting obligations are suspended or extended in the following circumstances:-

 Is in government custody

 Is outside of WA

 Is subject to an order as per division 6 of the CPOR Act; or  Is subject to an approval granted by the Commissioner of Police The period of which a ROs reporting obligations continue is extended by any length of time spent in government custody. As a case manager if you come across a ROs case file that requires suspension of reporting obligations please contact the Registry.

sections.h:
As a supervisor your roles and responsibilities entail the following in the case management of ROs and managing your case managers:-

 Provide coordination of NCOS relating to the management and monitoring of ROs within your sub-district

 Comply with standard operation procedures and ensure the integrity of information of ROs

 Develop effective inter-agency communication to provide information and assistance in matters relating to RO and DSO management  Promote strategies to assist your case managers to comply with police and legislation specific to the sex offender management processes  Manage a timely response to overdue reporting, IMS IR updates and Case Management Plans

 Ensure that sufficient staff are trained and have access to NCOS  Review and audit NCOS for the subdistrict to identify high risk ROs and ensure information upload is in the correct format and location  Review management process at a local level to ensure appropriate reporting regimes and case management plans are updated

As a Supervisor your level of access to NCOS is advances to a case manager’s access. This access level allows the manager to allocate NCOS case files to case managers and complete an audit and review function in NCOS. Supervisors should ensure new case managers have completed the relevant Blackboard Course/s to qualify for access to NCOS.

As a Supervisor, your access also allows the ability to revoke case file access from case managers. Revoking case file access occurs if the case manager has moved districts or is no longer performing the role as a case manager. To revoke access, click the box next the case managers name, then select the revoke option below and ‘save’.

TRANSFERRING REPORTABLE OFFENDERS – CASE FILES IN NCOS

All initiations of share or transfers of a NCOS case file must be performed by the Registry. The following circumstances may occur involving the Registry to transfer the NCOS case file:-

 RO changes address resulting in moving to a different district  RO enters WA from another jurisdiction of Australia

 RO enters from a foreign jurisdiction

 RO leaves WA for another jurisdiction of Australia

As a case manager you must ensure that the current NORO time, date and location are updated to reflect the new district. Whenever the case file is transferred to Page | 38

another district, the RO is to be informed of the appropriate police station to report to in the future.

As a supervisor you must ensure you either revoke or assign case managers access in NCOS. It is imperative that the supervisor advise the Registry who will arrange the share or transfer of the case file. If the RO resides and works in numerous districts, share access with the other districts can be initiated. This level of share is to be performed by the Registry.

sections.h:
The Registry generate district reports on a weekly basis detailing the list of overdue ROs. The five main categories of RO and NCOS include:-

Notification Required: ROs who have been sentenced and released from court or prison but have not been served with a P308. These ROs need to be served either a P308 or NORO to attend at the nearest police station for initial registration.

Registration Overdue: ROs who have been served a P308 upon release from court or prison but failed to attend at the nearest police station for initial registration within 7 days.

Overdue Reporting: ROs who have failed to report for their NOR appointments.

Absence Report Overdue: ROs who have failed to report return from their travel outside WA within 7 days.

Next Reporting Date Not Set: either the Registry has not received the signed NORO to set a new reporting date OR the RO will need service of a new NORO for their next reporting.

Within the weekly reports the Registry will also provide a list of ROs who are due for NORO reporting the following week. Included in this list are action items such as new photos required or those subject to outstanding warrants that can be addressed during the NORO interview.

Consorting Notice

If it is identified that two convicted child sex offenders are in contact, a consorting notice should be served on both offenders; unless they are a spouse, de facto, de facto child or lineal relative to the other person. A Consorting Notice advises a convicted child sex offender that another person is a convicted child sex offender, and habitually consorting with that person is an offence under s.557K (4) of the Criminal Code.

Consorting Notices can be served on convicted child sex offenders that do not fall under the scope of the Community Protection (Offender Reporting) Act 2004 and are not Reportable (Registered) Offenders.

Consorting Notices can be served on convicted child sex offenders who were juveniles at the time of offending; however, s.557K of the Criminal Code (Consorting Notices) will only be effective for two years from the completion of their sentence, pursuant to s189 of the Young Offenders Act 2004.

s.557K Criminal Code

(4) A person who is a child sex offender and who, having been warned by a police officer —

(a) that another person is also a child sex offender; and

(b) that consorting with the other person may lead to the person being charged with an offence

under this section, habitually consorts with the other person is guilty of an offence and is liable to imprisonment for 2 years and a fine of $24 000.

(5) It is a defence to a charge of an offence under subsection (4) to prove that the accused person —

(a) was the spouse or de facto partner of the other person; or

(b) was a de facto child or a lineal relative (as those terms are defined in section 329(1)) of the other

person.

s.329(1) Criminal Code

de facto child means a step-child of the offender or a child or step-child of a de facto partner of the offender;

lineal relative means a person who is a lineal ancestor, lineal descendant, brother, or sister, whether the relationship is of the whole blood or half-blood, whether or not the relationship is traced through, or to, a person whose parents were not married to each other at the time of the person’s birth, or subsequently, and whether the relationship is a natural relationship or a relationship established by a written law.

***Advice received from State Crime Solicitor Matthew Holgate, on 05/SEP/2016, indicates there is no definition of de facto partner in the Criminal Code - The Interpretation Act provision applies.

There is no requirement of 2 years residence for a relationship to satisfy the Interpretation Act definition of a de facto relationship. See File Note dated 05/SEP/2016 under NCOS case file 0037371 ***

s.13A Interpretation Act 1984

De facto relationship and de facto partner, references to

(1) A reference in a written law to a de facto relationship shall be construed as a reference to a

relationship (other than a legal marriage) between 2 persons who live together in a marriage-like relationship.

(2) The following factors are indicators of whether or not a de facto relationship exists between 2

persons, but are not essential —

Page | 69

(a) the length of the relationship between them;

(b) whether the 2 persons have resided together;

(c) the nature and extent of common residence;

(d) whether there is, or has been, a sexual relationship between them;

(e) the degree of financial dependence or interdependence, and any arrangements for financial

support, between them;

(f) the ownership, use and acquisition of their property (including property they own individually);

(g) the degree of mutual commitment by them to a shared life;

(h) whether they care for and support children;

(i) the reputation, and public aspects, of the relationship between them.

(3) It does not matter whether —

(a) the persons are different sexes or the same sex; or

(b) either of the persons is legally married to someone else or in another de facto relationship.

(4) A reference in a written law to a de facto partner shall be construed as a reference to a person

who lives, or where the context requires, has lived, in a de facto relationship.

(5) The de facto partner of a person (the first person) is the person who lives, or lived, in the de facto

relationship with the first person.

[Section 13A inserted by No. 3 of 2002 s. 85.]

A Consorting Notice advises a convicted child sex offender that another person is a convicted child sex offender, and habitually consorting with that person is an offence under s.557K (4) of the Criminal Code.
