sections.h:
Children of Interest

When adding a new Child of Interest to NCOS the relationship between the RO and the child and the nature of the contact (supervised/unsupervised) should always be listed in the comments section. Age and or DOB should be entered into the relevant sections.

As NCOS allows only one association i.e. “Lives with’ or ‘related to’ or ‘regularly with’, the preference is that ALL children living with a reportable offender (RO) have the association marked to indicate this.

It is imperative that the comment box within the child of interest details on NCOS be updated and a CPFS referral must be considered for all ROs living with children.

If the RO no longer has access to a particular child/children please tick the ‘No longer in contact’ box.

If a child/children have turned 18 years of age, please delete the details of that ‘child’ and add under associations.

CPFS Referrals

When a case manager identifies a possible child at risk, the provisions of section 28 of the Children and Community Services Act 2004, allows a referral to be made to

CPFS.

If a case manager is unsure about whether a particular child or children may be at risk of harm, they should discuss the matter further with their supervisor, the CPFS helpline or contact the Registry.

The referral should include a synopsis of all sexual convictions and sentences, details of the sexual offending including age of victim, relationship and nature of offences.

The referral should also include a synopsis of relevant violent convictions and sentence for CPFS to assess the risk.

CPFS referrals are to be forwarded to a NCOS authorised Inspector for approval, prior to releasing the information. Case managers at the Sex Offender Management Squad (SOMS) are to forward the drafted CPFS referrals to the Sex Offender Registry (SOR) SMAIL who will organise approval and release of information. Case managers in RWA are to forward the drafted CPFS referrals to their NCOS authorised Inspector within their district for approval prior to release of information.

For ROs residing in metropolitan and once approval has been sought from the authorised Inspector, SOR is then responsible for forwarding the referral to CPFS mailto:cpduty@communities.wa.gov.au to undertake a review of the welfare of the children named in the referral. SOR is also responsible of updating NCOS and following-up with the feedback relating to the possible children at risk.

Information that can be disclosed:-

 Personal details of child at risk

 Any occupants of the residence who are aware of ROs reporting obligations  Details of any immediate concerns you may have

 Details of previous convictions

Page | 75

 Statement of Material Facts

 Employment history

 Current victim’s details

 ROs living arrangements

 If RO and or victim are aware of the referral

 Reason for making a referral now

 Contact details of child at risks caregivers if they are aware of referral  Any special needs

Information not to be disclosed:-

 Information relating to the ROs risk assessment

 Information received from a third party

It should be specified whether the referral is a matter of ‘routine’ and if the information you are referring to CPFS is the result of specific information received.

In the relevant information section, it is also suggested that you state whether the information and the possible child or children at risk you are referring to is recent information and where that information originated from. e.g. ‘RO Bob SMITH has recently advised that he has moved into a residence with children’.

A copy of the signed referral must be uploaded to File Notes for future reference using the correct naming convention.

If a CPFS referral has been completed for a particular child/children, a comment stating ‘referral to CPFS submitted on DD/MM/YY’ must be added in the comments box for that particular child, within their association file child.

Page | 76

If a CPFS referral is not approved or decision made at management/supervisor’s level that it is not required, a comment stating ‘referral to CPFS not required at this time’ should be added in the comments box for that particular child, within their association file.

CPFS Referral Feedback in NCOS

Feedback from CPFS is essential to appropriately case manage a RO. It is also especially important if the RO reoffends and there is a contact sex review.

A procedure has been created for NCOS to alert SOR regarding the feedback process (as below) or lack of it.

This is only for SOMS initiated Child at Risk referrals to CPFS and NOT for CPFS S23 Request for Information. CPFS have advised SOMS that any action usually takes at least a month so SOR and SOMS case managers should expect our feedback after approximately 6 weeks. To ensure this occurs and is not forgotten, SOR will use NCOS to alert them when the 6 week period is up, enabling SOR to follow up any late/lost feedback and ensuring effective case management of the RO.

The procedure to follow is detailed below:-

 Complete the CPFS Child at Risk referral and forward the signed by Inspector copy to CPFS

 In NCOS under the Management tab under ‘Next Reporting Date’ tab - set a next reporting date on NCOS (Feedback required date) – this should be 6 weeks in advance from the date the referral was sent to CPFS Page | 77

Set by – Officers Surname & PD number

Action Type – should be WARNING

Comments box: Ensure the ‘naming conventions’ are used and worded as below:- When the entry has been completed it should look like the below example. It will differ from a NORO as the Type will be WARNING

Ensure an electronic copy of the CPFS referral has been saved into the offenders P Drive Folder and File Notes (or in the case of regional offices, in an appropriate location);

When the Feedback required date is imminent, NCOS will show the ‘PENDING REPORTING DATE’ alert to alert the case officer.

Page | 78

If feedback has NOT been obtained by the next reporting date (feedback date) the ‘OVERDUE REPORTING DATE RESULT’ will show on NCOS as below:- To have this alert removed follow up enquiries should be made with CPFS to ensure feedback is obtained.

The next reporting date can be further extended to reflect when this is expected.

If feedback is received remove ‘the bring up date’ in Next Reporting Date Details – go to the middle of the screen to the heading ‘Add Reporting Date Result and Close’ selecting the ‘Add’ button.

Page | 79

Complete the ‘Comments’ box with details of the date and where to locate the feedback.

The feedback is to be uploaded in ‘File Notes’ using the naming convention (NAME)

For regional WA if a visit is not practicable to check due to resources document this on the form. Forward the electronic copy to SOMS station mail. This completed document will allow proceedings for the RO to be published on the PSOR.

63. Failure to comply with reporting obligations

(1) A reportable offender who, without reasonable excuse, fails to comply with any of his or her

reporting obligations commits a crime.

Penalty: imprisonment for 5 years.

Summary conviction penalty: a fine of $12 000 and imprisonment for 2 years.

(2) In determining whether a person had a reasonable excuse for failing to comply with his or her

reporting obligations, a court must have regard to the following matters —

(a) the person’s age;

(b) Whether the person has a disability that affects the person’s ability to understand, or to

comply with, those obligations;

(c) Whether the form of notification given to the reportable offender as to his or her

Obligations was adequate to inform him or her of those obligations, having regard to The offender’s circumstances;

(d) Any matter specified by the regulations for the purposes of this section;

(e) Any other matter the court considers appropriate.

(3) It is a defence to proceedings for an offence of failing to comply with a reporting obligation if it is

established that, at the time the offence is alleged to have occurred, the person had not Received notice, and was otherwise unaware, of the obligation.

sections.t: Sample Smf for Failure to Comply with Reporting Obligations
sections.h:
**Request matter to be dealt with in a closed court due to accused's classification as a reportable offender.**

The accused in this matter is deemed a reportable offender under The Community Protection (Offender Reporting) Act 2004 (the Act), in relation to convictions of 1 x Indecently Deals with a Child Over 13 under 16 and 1 x Indecently dealt with a child in circumstances of aggravation. He was registered in accordance with the Act on (insert DATE) for a period of (insert reporting period of 7 years and 6 months OR 15 years OR life).

As a reportable offender, he was placed on the National Child Offender System (NCOS, formerly ANCOR) and required to report to police on a regular basis. During these meetings he is required to advise of certain changes to his personal circumstances within certain time frames of those changes occurring. The purpose of this reporting is to ensure police can accurately risk assess the reportable offender and take action where necessary to stop the reportable offender offending sexually against children.

On (insert DATE) the accused attended the Moora Police Station and was served a Notice of Reporting Obligations (NORO) by Sergeant Louden. The accused endorsed the new Notice of Reporting Obligations (NORO), acknowledging his obligations and for receiving a copy of that form. The NORO lists these obligations and reporting time frames on the rear.

One of these obligations is the accused must report within 24 hours any change of address in which he generally resides (being at that location for at least 7 days whether consecutive or not in any 12 month period). The accused failed to advise his change of address from ADDRESS, Moora where he had been residing for a few weeks.

EXPLANATION:

Page | 92

557K. Child sex offender, offences by

(1) In this section, unless the contrary intention appears —

child means a person under 18 years of age;

child sex offender means a person who has been convicted of —

(a) an offence under any of these Chapters of this Code that was committed

against, in respect of, or in the sight of, a child —

(i) Chapter XXII — Offences against morality;

(iia) Chapter XXV 6 — Child exploitation material;

(ii) Chapter XXXI — Sexual offences;

(iii) Chapter XXXIII — Offences against liberty;

or

(b) an offence under Chapter XXXIIIB that was committed against or in respect

of a child; or

(c) an offence under any of these repealed enactments of this Code that was

committed against a child —

(i) section 315 (Indecent assault on males);

(ii) Chapter XXXIA — Sexual assaults;

(iii) Chapter XXXII — Assaults on females: Abduction;

or

(d) an offence under section 59 of the Classification (Publications, Films and

Computer Games) Enforcement Act 1996 that was committed in circumstances in which an indecent or obscene article was sold, supplied or offered to a child; or

(e) an offence under the deleted section 60 of the Classification (Publications,

Films and Computer Games) Enforcement Act 1996; or

(f) an offence under section 101 of the Classification (Publications, Films and

Computer Games) Enforcement Act 1996 that was committed in circumstances in which —

(i) objectionable material was transmitted or demonstrated to a child; or

(ii) the objectionable material was child exploitation material (as defined

in section 217A 6);
